RoGER A. BROWN
I:wyer
38 North Washington Sheet
Post Office Box 475
Sonora, California 95370
May 28, 192
Fax (209) 533-7757
,
Phone (209) 533-7755
Wayne Imberi, Consultant
Technical Assistance and Analysis Division
Fair Political Practices Commtssion
428 J Stre€t, Suite 800
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807
RE: eotEgoalislof-Adyiee
Dear Wayne:
I appreciate very much your prompt and helpful advice today. While it is still fresh in my
mind, I wanted !o confirm your advice in writing to make sure I understand it corectly.
The facts are that A hired independent contractor B to perform research and analysis. B
prepared a written product. Neither A nor B had any interest in influencing legislative or
idministrative action at the time the ploduct was prepared, and it was prepared for an entirely
different pulpose.
Some time later, B was hired by C to make direct contacts to influence legislative action. B
realized the written product prepared for A would b9 gseful for the lobbying project and asked A for
pcrrnissi.ci^ to pro.,,ide b're p1rid,.ai -o a legiiiarivc ofScial. .{ saiC'$ere was no c'rjection trl usinE
the product in this way.
B was not a lobbyist or lobbying firm when the analysis, research and written product were
originally prepared. B registered as a lobbyist and lobbying firm on behalf of the new client, C.
niairect ion-t "t. 
and refinements of the written product after the first direct contact were paid for
bv c.
Under these facts, does A become a lobbyist employer by virtue of Regulation 18614? Is B
required to report on lobbying flrm reports compensation received from A for the research, analysis
and written pioduct which was ultimately provided to the legislative official?


Wayne Imberi, Consultant
Technical Assistance and Analysis Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 28, 1992
Page Two
Your oral advice was that neither A nor B were rcquried to report payments made or
received for the preparation of the written product. You explained, after consultation with Carla
Wardlaw and the kgal Division, that there is no disclosure responsibility so long as the initial
intent in creating the product was not for a lobbying purpose and so long as A is not authorizing use
of the product for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action.
Please confirm whether I have correctly stated your oral advice. We intend to follow your
advice and would tiiererbre appreciarc your confirmaiior as soox a; possibie. r)f cr.rursc, if i irave
misunderstood your advice, please let me know at once.
Thank you, again, for your prompt and courteous responses to my questions.
Very truly yours,
@-.P-*
RAB/ru:211d

sections.question: Under these facts, does A become a lobbyist employer by virtue of Regulation 18614? Is B required to report on lobbying firm reports compensation received from A for the research, analysis and written product which was ultimately provided to the legislative official?
sections.question_synthetic: Whether party A becomes a lobbyist employer and whether party B must report compensation from A for research and written products that were later used for lobbying purposes, when the product was originally created for a non-lobbying purpose.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request/confirmation letter from an attorney to FPPC, not an FPPC response letter. The attorney is summarizing oral advice received and requesting written confirmation. No formal CONCLUSION section exists. The letter references prior oral advice but does not contain the FPPC's written response/conclusion.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Under these facts, does A become a lobbyist employer by virtue of Regulation 18614? Is B required to report on lobbying firm reports compensation received from A for the research, analysis and written product which was ultimately provided to the legislative official?

embedding.summary: This is a follow-up letter from attorney Roger A. Brown to FPPC consultant Wayne Imberi confirming oral advice regarding lobbying disclosure obligations when a research product created for non-lobbying purposes is later used in a lobbying campaign.
